unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5: Money Matters. Money is a part of everyday life, but understanding how it works is a skill that takes practice. Whether you are saving coins in a jar at home, choosing between two things you want at the market, or hearing adults talk about bills and budgets, money is always around you. In this unit, you will explore the world of financial literacy — the ability to understand and use money wisely.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn the unit words a few at a time and say the example sentences aloud.
Work through the Grammar file. Focus especially on comparative adverbs, as they will appear throughout the unit.
Read the Story file, "The Notice Board", and answer the comprehension and discussion questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment to see what you have achieved.
Key Vocabulary
Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find the full word list with definitions and examples in your Vocabulary companion file. For now, read through these words and make sure you can say each one aloud.
Emotion and Behaviour Words
Growth and Change Words
Reading
Before you read
The passage below is about money, saving, and making good financial choices. It is written for young people your age who are starting to think about how money works. As you read, think about these questions:
What is the difference between a need and a want?
Why is saving money important, even when you are young?
How do families in your community talk about and use money?
Money, Markets, and Making Good Choices
Every morning in cities and towns across East Africa, markets come alive. Stalls are set up with colourful fabrics, fresh fruit, and bags of rice and sugar. Shopkeepers call out prices. Customers count their coins carefully. A woman buys enough flour for the week. A boy saves his last few shillings instead of buying sweets. A grandmother bargains firmly for a fair price on vegetables. Money moves from hand to hand, and each decision — big or small — tells a story about how people think about value.
But what is money, really? Money is a tool. It is not good or bad on its own. What matters is how we use it. Financial literacy means understanding how money works and learning to make smart choices with it. This includes knowing the difference between needs and wants. A need is something you must have to live safely and healthily, like food, clean water, shelter, and clothing. A want is something nice to have but not essential, like a new toy, a fancy phone case, or extra sweets after school.
One of the most crucial money skills is saving. Saving means putting aside part of your money for the future instead of spending it all at once. Even small amounts add up over time. If you save just a few coins each week, after several months you might have enough for something substantial — a book you have wanted, a gift for someone you love, or a contribution to a family goal. In Islam, we are taught to be balanced with money: not to waste it, not to hoard it selfishly, and to always share with those who have less. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that the best of people are those who are most beneficial to others. Giving sadaqah is not just generous — it is a way of purifying our wealth and strengthening our community.
A budget is a plan for how to use your money. Even young people can make a simple budget. Imagine you receive some pocket money each week. You could divide it into three parts: one part for saving, one part for spending on things you need or enjoy, and one part for giving to charity or helping your family. This simple plan helps you feel in control and teaches you to think productively about your resources. People who budget well are often more productive with their money than people who spend without thinking.
Another important skill is learning to compare prices. When you go to the market with your family, you might notice that one shop sells rice more cheaply than another, or that one brand of soap lasts longer than a cheaper one. Comparing carefully means you get better value. This is a form of critical thinking — the same skill you use in school when you adapt your ideas based on new information.
Money can sometimes cause stress, especially for families who do not have much. It is important to be sensitive to this. Not everyone has the same amount, and that is not something to feel uncomfortable about. What matters is being humble, grateful for what you have, and wise with whatever resources are available to you. A person who manages a meager income well can be far more successful than someone who wastes a large fortune.
Understanding money is a privilege and a responsibility. The earlier you start learning, the better prepared you will be. Whether you are saving for Eid, helping your family with shopping, or simply choosing between two treats at the corner shop, every choice you make is practice for the bigger decisions ahead.
Understanding the Text
Comprehension Questions
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage to find the evidence.
What happens every morning in markets across East Africa, according to the passage?
The passage says "Money is a tool." What does this mean?
What is the difference between a need and a want? Give one example of each from the text.
Why is saving important, even when you only have a small amount of money?
According to the passage, what three parts could you divide your pocket money into?
What does the passage say about comparing prices at the market?
Why does the passage say that a person with a meager income can be more successful than someone with a large fortune?
What Islamic teaching about money is mentioned in the passage? Explain it in your own words.
Vocabulary in Context
Find these words in the passage. Use the sentence around them to work out their meaning, then check with your Vocabulary file.
firmly (paragraph 1) — What does the grandmother do firmly? What does this word suggest about her?
crucial (paragraph 3) — "One of the most crucial money skills is saving." Why is this skill described as crucial?
substantial (paragraph 3) — What kind of thing might your savings become? What does substantial tell you about the amount?
sensitive (paragraph 6) — Why does the passage ask you to be sensitive about money?
meager (paragraph 6) — "A person who manages a meager income well..." What does meager tell you about the size of the income?
privilege (paragraph 7) — "Understanding money is a privilege and a responsibility." What does this mean?
Deeper Thinking
These questions ask you to think beyond what the passage says directly. There is no single right answer, but you should explain your thinking clearly.
The passage says that money "is not good or bad on its own." Do you agree? Can you think of times when money causes problems, and times when it helps?
The passage connects saving money with Islamic values. How do your family or community values shape the way you think about spending and saving?
The passage talks about the difference between needs and wants. Sometimes this is not easy to decide. Give an example of something that might be a need for one person but a want for another. Explain your thinking.
The passage says that "every choice you make is practice for the bigger decisions ahead." What bigger financial decisions might you face when you are older? How does practising now help?
Think about the grandmother bargaining at the market. Why is it important to know the fair price of things? What could happen if people did not compare prices?
Grammar Focus: Comparative Adverbs
In the reading passage, you met sentences that compared actions — for example, one shop sells rice *more cheaply* than another, or one person manages money *better* than another. These comparisons use comparative adverbs. Understanding comparative adverbs is one of the most important grammar skills in this unit, because they help you write clear, precise comparisons in your opinion essays and everyday conversations.
What are comparative adverbs?
An adverb is a word that tells us how, when, or where something happens. For example, *quickly*, *slowly*, *firmly*, and *eagerly* are all adverbs. A comparative adverb is the form we use when we want to compare how two actions are done.
For example:
"Hannah spoke more firmly than before." (We are comparing how she spoke at two different times.)
"He runs faster than his brother." (We are comparing the speed of two runners.)
"She adapted more quickly than I did." (We are comparing how quickly two people adapted.)
How to form comparative adverbs
The rule depends on the length of the adverb:
Rule 1: Short adverbs (one syllable) — add -er
fast → faster: "He runs faster than his sister."
hard → harder: "She works harder than anyone in the class."
late → later: "They arrived later than expected."
loud → louder: "The teacher spoke louder so everyone could hear."
Rule 2: Longer adverbs (two or more syllables) — add "more" before the adverb
quickly → more quickly: "She finished her homework more quickly than I did."
carefully → more carefully: "He counted the coins more carefully than before."
eagerly → more eagerly: "The children waited more eagerly for Eid than for any other holiday."
dramatically → more dramatically: "The weather changed more dramatically in the mountains."
firmly → more firmly: "She held the rope more firmly after it nearly slipped."
Rule 3: Irregular comparative adverbs
Some adverbs do not follow the regular rules. You need to learn these by heart:
well → better: "She sings better than her friend."
badly → worse: "He did worse on the second test than the first."
far → farther (or further): "We walked farther than we planned."
little → less: "He complained less after he understood the reason."
much → more: "She practised more than anyone else."
Always use "than"
When you compare two things using a comparative adverb, you almost always use the word than to connect them:
"She speaks English more confidently than she did last year."
"He saves money more wisely than his older brother."
"The market opens earlier than the shops on the high street."
A common mistake and how to avoid it
Mistake: Adding "-er" to a long adverb instead of using "more".
Wrong: "She runs quicklier than me." ✘
Right: "She runs more quickly than me." ✔
Mistake: Forgetting "than" in a comparison.
Wrong: "He works harder I do." ✘
Right: "He works harder than I do." ✔
Memory trick: If the adverb is a short word (one syllable), just add -er. If it is a longer word (two or more syllables), put more in front. Think of "more" as your helper for longer words.
Grammar Focus: Quantifiers
A quantifier is a word that tells us how much or how many of something there is. Quantifiers are useful whenever you need to talk about amounts, whether you are describing people, objects, or ideas. In this unit, you will use quantifiers when writing about money, budgets, and the choices people make.
Common quantifiers and how to use them
Here are the quantifiers you need to know:
both — used for exactly two things or people: "Both students saved their pocket money."
neither — used for two things to say not one of them: "Neither answer was negative."
some — a number of, but not all: "Some children prefer to spend rather than save."
many — a large number of: "Many families in our community give to charity regularly."
few — a small number of: "Only a few students forgot their homework."
a lot of — a large amount: "There are a lot of stalls at the market."
all — every one: "All the coins were counted carefully."
each — every individual one: "Each student received pocket money for the week."
every — all, without exception: "Every choice you make with money is practice."
Both vs. Neither
These two quantifiers are used specifically for groups of two:
"Both of the students had fabulous ideas." (Two students, and each one had ideas.)
"Neither of the two answers was correct." (Two answers, and not one of them was correct.)
Important: Both takes a plural verb ("Both students were ready"), but neither takes a singular verb ("Neither student was ready").
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using "both" with a singular noun.
Wrong: "Both student had ideas." ✘
Right: "Both students had ideas." ✔
Mistake: Using "neither" with "not".
Wrong: "Neither of them did not come." ✘ (Double negative.)
Right: "Neither of them came." ✔
Grammar Focus: Reflexive Pronouns
A reflexive pronoun is a special pronoun we use when the person doing the action and the person receiving the action are the same. Reflexive pronouns always end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). They are important for clear, accurate English, and they appear often in the story "The Notice Board" and in everyday conversation.
The reflexive pronouns
Here is the complete list:
I → myself: "I taught myself how to save money."
you (one person) → yourself: "Did you prepare yourself for the test?"
he → himself: "He made the bed by himself."
she → herself: "She reminded herself to be patient."
it → itself: "The cat cleaned itself after playing."
we → ourselves: "We enjoyed ourselves at the Eid celebration."
you (more than one) → yourselves: "Did you all prepare yourselves for the trip?"
they → themselves: "They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Two uses of reflexive pronouns
Use 1: When the subject and object are the same person.
This is the main use. The subject does the action to themselves:
"She looked at herself in the mirror." (She looked at her own reflection.)
"I cut myself while cooking." (I accidentally cut my own hand.)
"They taught themselves to budget." (Nobody else taught them; they learned alone.)
Use 2: For emphasis (to stress that someone did something alone or without help).
"I baked the cake myself." (I did it without anyone helping me.)
"Hannah wrote her name on the board herself." (She did it personally, not someone else.)
"The children organised the event themselves." (No adult helped them.)
Common mistakes
Mistake: Using a reflexive pronoun when the subject and object are different people.
Wrong: "She told myself the news." ✘
Right: "She told me the news." ✔ (She and me are different people.)
Mistake: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Wrong: "He taught hisself to cook." ✘
Right: "He taught himself to cook." ✔
Memory trick: Think of reflexive pronouns as "self words." If the action bounces back to the same person who did it, like a ball bouncing off a wall, you need a reflexive pronoun.
Grammar Practice
Practice 1: Comparative adverbs — choose the correct form
Read each sentence and choose the correct comparative adverb. Write the full sentence in your notebook.
She solved the maths problem (more quickly / quicklier) than her classmate.
He speaks Arabic (more fluently / fluenter) than English.
The bus arrived (later / more lately) than we expected.
Sophia adapted to school life (more easily / easilier) than she thought she would.
He runs (faster / more fast) than anyone in his year group.
My sister saves money (more carefully / carefullier) than I do.
The weather changed (more dramatically / dramaticallier) in the afternoon.
She performed (better / more good) in the second test than the first.
Practice 2: Quantifiers — fill in the blank
Choose the correct quantifier from the options in brackets.
______ of the two brothers saved their pocket money. (Both / All)
______ student in the class received a pencil. (Every / Many)
______ of the answers on the quiz were correct. (Neither / A lot of)
There are ______ reasons to learn about money. (many / every)
______ children like to save, while others prefer to spend. (Some / Each)
She checked ______ item on her shopping list. (every / some)
______ of the two friends could attend the party. (Neither / Few)
There were ______ people at the market this morning. (a lot of / each)
Practice 3: Reflexive pronouns — complete the sentences
Fill in each blank with the correct reflexive pronoun.
She taught ______ how to make a budget.
We organised the charity sale by ______.
He accidentally hurt ______ while cutting the paper.
"I will do it ______," said Hannah.
The cat washed ______ after playing in the mud.
Did you all enjoy ______ at the school fair?
They prepared ______ for the big exam.
I reminded ______ to save part of my pocket money.
Practice 4: Mixed grammar — spot and fix the mistakes
Each sentence has one grammar error. Find it and rewrite the sentence correctly.
She runs more fastly than her brother. (Think: is "fastly" a real word?)
Both student agreed to share the prize. (Think: should this be singular or plural?)
He taught hisself to ride a bicycle. (Think: what is the correct reflexive pronoun?)
Neither of the girls were chosen for the team. (Think: does "neither" take a singular or plural verb?)
I saved money more better than last month. (Think: is "better" already comparative?)
She told myself the answer. (Think: are "she" and "I" the same person?)
Answers
Practice 1: 1. more quickly  2. more fluently  3. later  4. more easily  5. faster  6. more carefully  7. more dramatically  8. better
Practice 2: 1. Both  2. Every  3. Neither  4. many  5. Some  6. every  7. Neither  8. a lot of
Practice 3: 1. herself  2. ourselves  3. himself  4. myself  5. itself  6. yourselves  7. themselves  8. myself
Practice 4: 1. She runs faster than her brother. ("Fast" is already short; just add -er.)  2. Both students agreed to share the prize. ("Both" needs a plural noun.)  3. He taught himself to ride a bicycle. (Not "hisself".)  4. Neither of the girls was chosen for the team. ("Neither" takes a singular verb.)  5. I saved money better than last month. (Do not add "more" before an already irregular comparative.)  6. She told me the answer. ("She" and "I" are different people, so no reflexive pronoun.)

readings.title: Money Matters: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 5: Money Matters. Money is a part of everyday life, but understanding how it works is a skill that takes practice. Whether you are saving coins in a jar at home, choosing between two things you want at the market, or hearing adults talk about bills and budgets, money is always around you. In this unit, you will explore the world of financial literacy — the ability to understand and use money wisely.
You will read about spending and saving, think about the value of money, and discuss what it means to be responsible with the things we have. You will learn about comparative adverbs (words that compare how actions are done, such as *more quickly* or *faster*), quantifiers (words that tell us how much or how many, such as *both*, *neither*, *some*, and *many*), and reflexive pronouns (words like *myself*, *herself*, and *themselves* that show when someone does something to or for themselves). These grammar tools will help you write a strong opinion essay about pocket money and saving by the end of the unit.
Alongside this lesson, you will read the story "The Notice Board", set in a boarding school in southern Africa. The story follows a girl named Hannah who is new at school and must find the courage to speak up, adapt to challenges, and stand for fairness. The story explores themes of personal growth, emotional strength, and speaking up for what is right. It will help you think about responsibility, self-awareness, and how our choices shape who we become.
In many Somali and East African families, children learn about money from a young age. You might help count change at a shop, save for Eid, or hear family discussions about halal ways of earning and spending. Islam teaches us to be neither wasteful nor stingy, to give sadaqah (charity), and to be grateful for what we have. These are powerful financial principles. This unit will help you connect those values with the skills of budgeting, saving, and making thoughtful choices about money.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand texts about spending, saving, and financial literacy.
Use comparative adverbs correctly to compare actions (e.g. *more quickly*, *faster*, *better*).
Use quantifiers to talk about amounts (e.g. *both*, *neither*, *some*, *many*, *a lot of*).
Use reflexive pronouns correctly to show when someone does something to or for themselves (e.g. *myself*, *herself*, *themselves*).
Write a structured opinion essay about pocket money, saving, or spending wisely.
Use connecting words to link ideas in your writing (e.g. *firstly*, *however*, *therefore*, *finally*).

readings.title: Money Matters: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Speak clearly when presenting your opinion or discussing money habits.
Think critically about smart spending, the value of money, and the difference between needs and wants.
Understand and enjoy the story "The Notice Board" and explore themes of courage, fairness, and personal growth.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn the unit words a few at a time and say the example sentences aloud.
Work through the Grammar file. Focus especially on comparative adverbs, as they will appear throughout the unit.
Read the Story file, "The Notice Board", and answer the comprehension and discussion questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment to see what you have achieved.
Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find the full word list with definitions and examples in your Vocabulary companion file. For now, read through these words and make sure you can say each one aloud.
The passage below is about money, saving, and making good financial choices. It is written for young people your age who are starting to think about how money works. As you read, think about these questions:
Money, Markets, and Making Good Choices
Every morning in cities and towns across East Africa, markets come alive. Stalls are set up with colourful fabrics, fresh fruit, and bags of rice and sugar. Shopkeepers call out prices. Customers count their coins carefully. A woman buys enough flour for the week. A boy saves his last few shillings instead of buying sweets. A grandmother bargains firmly for a fair price on vegetables. Money moves from hand to hand, and each decision — big or small — tells a story about how people think about value.
But what is money, really? Money is a tool. It is not good or bad on its own. What matters is how we use it. Financial literacy means understanding how money works and learning to make smart choices with it. This includes knowing the difference between needs and wants. A need is something you must have to live safely and healthily, like food, clean water, shelter, and clothing. A want is something nice to have but not essential, like a new toy, a fancy phone case, or extra sweets after school.
One of the most crucial money skills is saving. Saving means putting aside part of your money for the future instead of spending it all at once. Even small amounts add up over time. If you save just a few coins each week, after several months you might have enough for something substantial — a book you have wanted, a gift for someone you love, or a contribution to a family goal. In Islam, we are taught to be balanced with money: not to waste it, not to hoard it selfishly, and to always share with those who have less. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that the best of people are those who are most beneficial to others. Giving sadaqah is not just generous — it is a way of purifying our wealth and strengthening our community.

readings.title: The Notice Board, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The first day at Mbali Academy was not what Hannah expected.
She had been eagerly waiting to move into the school dormitory for weeks. Her mother said it would help her become more independent. Her father, who rarely shared emotions, simply hugged her and said, "Remember who you are."
Sophia was not sure what that meant. But she promised herself she would try.
Mbali Academy was a massive, stone-walled school perched on a green ridge in southern Africa. It was known for its fabulous science program and its strict rules. Students wore uniforms and ate in long rows. The dorms were clean but cold.
Her roommate was a girl named Amal, who talked firmly and fast.
"Don't touch my shelf," Amal said the moment Hannah entered.
She unpacked her meager belongings — two books, a tiny photo frame, and a borrowed sweater. Compared to Amal's colourful bedding and shiny decorations, Hannah's side looked bare.
At dinner, Hannah tried to talk to others. But most were already in their own friend groups. She felt a little uncomfortable, like a chair with one short leg.
"Just give it time," she told herself.
Later that week, a teacher announced the new student council would be formed. "One student will be chosen from each dorm group. They will help represent your voices and ideas."
Sophia's ears perked up. Maybe this was her chance to be part of something.
The next morning, Hannah saw a poster on the school notice board.
"Nominate a student who shows leadership, kindness, and courage."
Amal and her friends giggled behind her.
"We already know who will win," Amal said. "Nadia. Everyone likes her."
Sophia looked at the names scribbled on the board. All were from the same friend group. None were new students. None were like her.
"I don't belong," she whispered, frustrated.
But something inside her — small but substantial — told her to speak.
She wrote her name in shaky handwriting.
"Who is that girl?" someone asked.
"Hannah," another replied. "Isn't she the one with the second-hand sweater?"
Amal rolled her eyes. "That's a questionable choice."
Sophia felt heat in her chest. She thought about removing her name. But a teacher passed by and said, "I saw your name. Good for you."
The council speeches were set for Friday. Students would gather in the auditorium. Each candidate had to give a short talk.
Sophia felt like her stomach had flipped. She barely slept Thursday night.
"You don't have to do this," Amal said in the morning. "It's not too late to back out."
Sophia looked at her reflection in the mirror. "I didn't come here to hide," she said quietly.

readings.title: The Notice Board, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
She remembered what her father had said.
When it was her turn to speak, Hannah walked slowly to the front. Her paper trembled in her hands.
"My name is Hannah. I'm new here, and I know I don't have a big group of friends yet. But I believe kindness should be a rule, not a suggestion."
"Sometimes rules feel unavoidable, but that doesn't make them right. Some are even unconstitutional — they go against fairness."
"I've felt frustrated and small here. But I've also learned that even small voices can grow. I will do my best to be fair, hospitable, and honest."
She stepped back. Her knees felt wobbly.
The applause was quiet — but real.
Later, someone tapped her shoulder. A younger girl from another dorm.
"Thank you," the girl said. "I felt sensitive when I arrived too. You made me feel seen."
Sophia smiled wide. It was not a massive moment, but it felt crucial.
Voting happened the next day. When the results were posted, Hannah was not the winner. Nadia had the most votes.
But below her name, another line had been added:
"Hannah: assistant rep for dorm 2."
Sophia nodded. "Maybe the rules adapted."
In the weeks that followed, she helped organise weekend activities, sorted complaints, and reminded teachers when supplies were unavailable. She took the job seriously and learned quickly.
She noticed that when she spoke firmly, people listened.
When she spoke aggressively, they pushed back.
When she spoke dramatically, they laughed.
She started choosing her tone carefully.
One day, a rule was posted on the notice board: "Only students with full uniforms may attend weekend outings."
Several students couldn't afford new shoes. Hannah raised her hand in the council meeting.
"This rule," she said, "feels unfortunate and unfair. Some students try their best but don't have the means."
"It's about order," said a teacher.
"But order should never come before compassion," Hannah replied.
Another rep whispered, "That rule sounds… unconstitutional."
They voted. The rule was changed.
Sophia felt her heart flutter. Change could be slow — but it was real.
One evening, she and Amal sat on their beds, both doing homework.
"You've changed," Amal said. "You used to be so quiet."
"I had to adapt," Hannah replied. "And you?"
Amal shrugged. "I'm learning too. Being right all the time doesn't mean being respected."
They shared a laugh. It was small — but felt honest.
During essay week, Hannah wrote a piece called "Growing From the Middle."
She used words like productive, symbiotic, and fragile.
"A school is like a tree," she wrote.
"It needs deep roots of respect, branches of growth, and leaves that fall and come again. Every person here, whether popular or unpopular, makes it grow. Sometimes a fragile moment becomes the start of strength."

grammar.title: Comparative Adverbs
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. They tell us if something is done in a greater or lesser way than something else. This is important because it helps us describe differences in actions, making our conversations and writing clearer and more interesting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Comparative adverbs help us compare how actions are done. They tell us if something is done in a greater or lesser way than something else. This is important because it helps us describe differences in actions, making our conversations and writing clearer and more interesting.
For example, when we say "She adapted more quickly than I did," we are comparing how quickly she adapted to how quickly I adapted. This helps us understand that her action was done at a faster rate.
How to form comparative adverbs (step by step)
Identify the adverb you want to compare. Adverbs often tell us how, when, or where something happens. Examples include *quickly*, *slowly*, *easily*, and *happily*.
Decide how to make it comparative:
For short adverbs (one syllable), add -er:
fast → faster: "He runs faster than his brother."
hard → harder: "She studies harder than before."
late → later: "They arrived later than expected."
For longer adverbs (two or more syllables), use more before the adverb:
quickly → more quickly: "She adapted more quickly than I did."
carefully → more carefully: "He counted the money more carefully this time."
eagerly → more eagerly: "The students waited more eagerly for the results."
For irregular adverbs, learn the special forms:
well → better: "She sings better than her sister."
badly → worse: "He feels worse today than yesterday."
far → farther / further: "We walked farther than planned."
Make the comparison with "than"
Always use than to show what you are comparing:
"She speaks English more confidently than she did last year."
"He runs faster than his sister."
"She dances more gracefully than anyone else."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using "-er" with adverbs that have two or more syllables.
Wrong: "She runs quicklier than me." ✘
Right: "She runs more quickly than me." ✔
Mistake 2: Forgetting to use "than" when making a comparison.
Wrong: "He runs faster I do." ✘
Right: "He runs faster than I do." ✔
Quick memory trick
One syllable? Just add -er (fast → faster). Two or more syllables? Use more (quickly → more quickly). Think of "more" as your helper for longer words!
Practice 1: Fill in the blanks
Write the correct comparative form of the adverb in brackets.
My brother plays video games ______ (badly) than I do.
She solved the maths problem ______ (quickly) than I expected.
He speaks ______ (softly) than his friend.
The bus arrived ______ (late) than usual.
She sings ______ (beautifully) than anyone in the choir.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply comparative adverbs in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Quantifiers
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Quantifiers are words that tell us how much or how many of something there is. They help us understand the quantity of nouns, which can be really useful in both speaking and writing. For example, instead of saying "There are students," we can say "Both of the students had fabulous ideas." This makes our sentences clearer and more informative.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Quantifiers are words that tell us how much or how many of something there is. They help us understand the quantity of nouns, which can be really useful in both speaking and writing. For example, instead of saying "There are students," we can say "Both of the students had fabulous ideas." This makes our sentences clearer and more informative.
How to use quantifiers (step by step)
Identify the noun you want to describe. For example, in the sentence "Both of the students had fabulous ideas," the noun is "students."
Choose the right quantifier: Use "both" for two items. Use "neither" for two items to say not one of them. Use "some," "many," "few," and "a lot of" for larger groups.
Place the quantifier before the noun: "both students" or "neither answer."
Make sure it matches: "Both" needs a plural noun and plural verb. "Neither" needs a singular verb.
Examples
"Both of the students had fabulous ideas."
"Neither answer was negative."
"Some children like to play outside."
"Many animals live in the forest."
"A lot of people attended the concert."
"Each student received a book."
"Every family has different traditions."
"Only a few students were absent today."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using singular with "both".
Wrong: "Both student had ideas." ✘
Right: "Both students had ideas." ✔
Mistake 2: Mixing up "both" and "neither".
"Both" means yes, both of them. "Neither" means no, not one of them. Make sure you use the right one for your meaning.
Quick memory trick
Think of "both" as a pair (like shoes) that always comes together, and "neither" as a way to say "not either of two." Remember: both means "yes, both," and neither means "no, neither one."
Practice 2: Fill in the blanks
Use the correct quantifier in the blanks.
______ of the answers were correct. (both / neither)
I saw ______ cats in the garden. (some / many)
______ of the two friends could come to the party. (Both / Neither)
There are ______ apples in the basket. (few / a lot of)
______ student must bring their own pencil. (Each / Many)
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply quantifiers in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Reflexive Pronouns
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject of a sentence does the action to themselves. In English, we have these reflexive pronouns: *myself*, *yourself*, *himself*, *herself*, *itself*, *ourselves*, *yourselves*, and *themselves*. Understanding reflexive pronouns is important because it helps you express actions in a clear way, especially when you want to emphasise that someone is doing something for themselves.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Reflexive pronouns are special words we use when the subject of a sentence does the action to themselves. In English, we have these reflexive pronouns: *myself*, *yourself*, *himself*, *herself*, *itself*, *ourselves*, *yourselves*, and *themselves*. Understanding reflexive pronouns is important because it helps you express actions in a clear way, especially when you want to emphasise that someone is doing something for themselves.
How to form and use reflexive pronouns (step by step)
Identify the subject: Look for who is doing the action in the sentence.
Choose the right reflexive pronoun: Match the pronoun to the subject: I → myself, you → yourself, he → himself, she → herself, it → itself, we → ourselves, they → themselves.
Place the reflexive pronoun correctly: Put the reflexive pronoun after the verb or at the end of the sentence. Example: "They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Examples
"I taught myself how to ride a bike."
"She looked at herself in the mirror."
"We enjoyed ourselves at the party."
"He made the bed by himself."
"They prepared themselves for the challenge."
Emphasis use
Sometimes reflexive pronouns are used for emphasis — to highlight that someone did something alone or without help:
"I made the cake myself." (This highlights that you did it alone, not with help.)
"She solved the problem herself." (Nobody helped her.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong reflexive pronoun.
Wrong: "He taught hisself to cook." ✘
Right: "He taught himself to cook." ✔
Mistake 2: Using a reflexive pronoun when it is not needed.
Wrong: "She enjoyed herself the movie." ✘
Right: "She enjoyed the movie." ✔ (The subject and object are different things here.)
Quick memory trick
Think of reflexive pronouns as "self" words! If the action bounces back to the same person who did it, you need a reflexive pronoun. I → myself, you → yourself, he → himself, she → herself, it → itself, we → ourselves, they → themselves.
Practice 3: Fill in the blanks
Choose the correct reflexive pronoun to complete each sentence.
She taught ______ how to dance.
We had a great time by ______ at the park.
He fixed the computer by ______.
The cat cleaned ______ after playing.
I reminded ______ to save my pocket money.
They prepared ______ for the school trip.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

grammar.title: Reported Speech (Introduction)
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is when we tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. For example, instead of saying "I am happy," we can say "She said she was happy." Understanding reported speech is important because it helps us share information while keeping the conversation flowing smoothly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Reported speech, also known as indirect speech, is when we tell someone what another person said without quoting their exact words. For example, instead of saying "I am happy," we can say "She said she was happy." Understanding reported speech is important because it helps us share information while keeping the conversation flowing smoothly.
How to form reported speech (step by step)
Identify the original statement: For example, "I am frustrated by the change."
Change the pronouns: "I" changes to "she" or "he" depending on who is speaking.
Change the tense: Usually, move back one tense. "am" (present) changes to "was" (past).
Remove quotation marks: When we report speech, we do not use quotation marks.
Add a reporting verb: Use a reporting verb like "said" or "told."
Putting it all together: She said she was frustrated by the change.
Examples
Direct: "I love ice cream." → Reported: She said she loved ice cream.
Direct: "We are going to the park." → Reported: They said they were going to the park.
Direct: "Can you help me?" → Reported: He asked if I could help him.
Direct: "Please finish your homework." → Reported: The teacher told us to finish our homework.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Not changing the tense.
Wrong: She said she is tired. ✘
Right: She said she was tired. ✔
Mistake 2: Using quotation marks in reported speech.
Wrong: She said, "she was frustrated." ✘
Right: She said she was frustrated. ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of it like telling a story! When you report speech, you are sharing what someone else said, like you are telling a story. Just remember to change the words a little to make it fit your story.
Practice 4: Transform direct speech into reported speech
Direct: "I will help you." → Reported: She said ________.
Direct: "I am reading a book." → Reported: He said ________.
Direct: "Do you like pizza?" → Reported: She asked ________.
Direct: "We are saving money for Eid." → Reported: They said ________.

grammar.title: Connecting Words
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Connecting words help us link ideas together smoothly in our writing and speaking. They make our sentences clearer and our thoughts easier to understand. By using connecting words, we can show how one idea relates to another, which is essential for good communication, especially when writing opinion essays.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Connecting words help us link ideas together smoothly in our writing and speaking. They make our sentences clearer and our thoughts easier to understand. By using connecting words, we can show how one idea relates to another, which is essential for good communication, especially when writing opinion essays.
The main connecting words
Firstly — to introduce the first point: "Firstly, saving money teaches patience."
However — to show a contrast or exception: "I wanted to save; however, I spent it on snacks."
Therefore — to show a result or conclusion: "She saved every week; therefore, she had enough for Eid."
In addition / Furthermore — to add another point: "In addition, budgeting helps families."
Finally — to indicate the last point: "Finally, I want to say that saving is for everyone."
For example / For instance — to give an example: "For example, you could save five coins a week."
In conclusion — to sum up: "In conclusion, financial literacy is a crucial skill."
How to use them in sentences
Connecting words usually go at the beginning of a sentence or clause, followed by a comma:
"Firstly, we need to gather our materials."
"I wanted to go outside; however, it started to rain."
"She studied hard; therefore, she passed with flying colours."
"Finally, we reached our destination after a long journey."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Forgetting the comma after the connecting word.
Wrong: "However I don't eat it often." ✘
Right: "However, I don't eat it often." ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of connecting words as bridges between two ideas. Just like a bridge helps you cross from one side to another, connecting words help your thoughts flow smoothly!
Practice 5: Fill in the blanks
Fill in the blanks with the correct connecting word: *Firstly, However, Therefore, Finally, In addition*.
I woke up late this morning. ______, I missed the bus.
______, we will go over the rules of the game.
I studied for the test. ______, I felt confident when I took it.
We had a lot of fun at the party. ______, we danced all night!
Saving money is important. ______, it teaches us to plan for the future.

grammar.title: Tone Awareness
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Tone awareness is about understanding how the words we choose can change the way our message feels to the reader or listener. Different words can make a sentence sound exciting, serious, or even friendly. When we write persuasively (like in an opinion essay), choosing the right tone helps us communicate our feelings clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Tone awareness is about understanding how the words we choose can change the way our message feels to the reader or listener. Different words can make a sentence sound exciting, serious, or even friendly. When we write persuasively (like in an opinion essay), choosing the right tone helps us communicate our feelings clearly.
How to use tone (step by step)
Identify the emotion: Think about how you want your reader to feel. Do you want them to feel excited, serious, or motivated?
Choose the right words: Use adverbs that match the feeling you want. For example: eagerly (excited), firmly (serious), dramatically (surprising).
Use the words in sentences: Place your chosen words in your sentences. Make sure they fit well.
Revise for tone: After writing, read your sentences out loud. Do they sound like the tone you intended?
Examples from the story
In "The Notice Board," Hannah learns that tone matters:
When she spoke firmly, people listened. (Serious and confident tone.)
When she spoke aggressively, they pushed back. (Harsh and forceful tone.)
When she spoke dramatically, they laughed. (Over-the-top tone.)
This shows that the same message can have very different effects depending on the tone we use.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using a word that does not match the tone.
Wrong: "The puppy firmly wagged its tail." (Too serious for a playful puppy.)
Right: "The puppy happily wagged its tail." ✔
Quick memory trick
Think of tone as the "mood" of your writing. Just like music can be happy or sad, your words can create different feelings. Choose your words like you choose your music!
Practice 6: Fill in the blanks
Complete the sentences using *dramatically*, *firmly*, or *eagerly*.
The chef ______ presented her new dish to the judges, hoping to impress them.
The teacher spoke ______ when explaining the importance of studying.
The puppy bounced ______ when it saw its owner coming home.
The actor ______ dropped to one knee during the play.
The student spoke ______ during the debate, making her point clear.

writing.title: Writing 1: Opinion Essay on Pocket Money and Saving
writing.promptAndInstructions:
An opinion essay is a piece of writing in which you share what you think about a topic and give reasons to support your view. In this unit, your topic is money — specifically, pocket money and saving. A good opinion essay does not just say "I think saving is good." It explains why you think so, gives examples, and uses connecting words to guide the reader through your argument.
The structure of an opinion essay
A well-organised opinion essay has four parts:
Introduction: State the topic and your opinion clearly. For example: "I believe that every young person should learn to save money, because saving teaches us patience and responsibility."
First reason with evidence: Give your strongest reason. Explain it with an example or detail. Use the connecting word Firstly to begin.
Second reason with evidence: Give another reason. Use In addition or Furthermore to connect it to the first.
Conclusion: Summarise your opinion and leave the reader with a strong final thought. Use In conclusion or Therefore to signal the end.
Useful language for opinion essays
Here are phrases you can use in your essay. Practise saying them aloud.
Introducing your opinion:
"I believe that..."
"In my opinion,..."

quizzes.question: What does 'humble' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily affected by things around you, like feelings or physical things. | In a strong and steady way; not easily moved or changed. | Not enough; very small in amount or quality. | Not thinking that you are better than others; modest.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not thinking that you are better than others; modest.
quizzes.explanation: Even though he won the race, he remained humble and thanked his friends for their support.

quizzes.question: What does 'eagerly' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily affected by things around you, like feelings or physical things. | Feeling uneasy or not relaxed. | In a strong and steady way; not easily moved or changed. | In a way that shows you really want to do something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: In a way that shows you really want to do something.
quizzes.explanation: She eagerly opened her birthday presents.

quizzes.question: What does 'meager' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily affected by things around you, like feelings or physical things. | Feeling uneasy or not relaxed. | Not enough; very small in amount or quality. | Something that is not certain or may not be true.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not enough; very small in amount or quality.
quizzes.explanation: The meal was meager, with only a small piece of bread and a little bit of soup.

quizzes.question: What does 'firmly' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling uneasy or not relaxed. | Feeling upset because you cannot do something you want to do. | In a strong and steady way; not easily moved or changed. | Something that is not certain or may not be true.
quizzes.correctAnswer: In a strong and steady way; not easily moved or changed.
quizzes.explanation: She held the rope firmly so it would not slip from her hands.
